data.title: Top Dividend Funds: JEPQ Vs. QQA For Monthly Income Up To 10%
data.description:
Two great monthly payers

As I start to transition into a full-time writer and an individual partially living off of my investments, finding stable ways to beat money market funds and still maintain reliable liquidity has been top of mind. The recent trends in hybrid index funds that have both a stock segment [in larger proportionality] and an options segment have become very popular, especially as rates on money markets and high-yield accounts begin to trend downward.

This has also been a 3-month investigation into figuring out how all of these funds work, as they are all a little bit different. I've made calls to JPMorgan and Invesco and really appreciate all the help they have provided me with to understand these products. Today I will compare two excellent funds, the Invesco QQQ Income Advantage ETF (NASDAQ:QQA) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ).

In my opinion, both are great options to maintain exposure to the market and have a source of liquidity that shouldn't jump all over the place when you need to sell shares to pay expenses. These are all the things I'm looking for, and the strategy has begun to make more sense the more I study these two sets of funds.

I also want to emphasize that this is not a strategy I'm taking for my portfolio broadly. I have stocks and ETFs that I hold and plan to hold for as long as possible. To me, these funds are where I would like to start moving some of my money market positions to, positions that I would use to supplement income through share sales.

Here are some of the differences between these two monthly payers

Here is a bullet point list of the differences and similarities between these two funds:

  • QQA is managed by Invesco [the creator of The Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) and The Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP)].
  • JEPQ uses a proprietary stock-picking strategy to emulate the Nasdaq 100 and attempt to provide alpha to the strategy.
  • QQA uses QQQ as an index to replicate the index fund exactly for the stock proportion of this fund. This is a true index fund hybrid versus a beta plus index emulator.
  • JEPQ writes covered calls for the income portion of the portfolio against Nasdaq futures, the strategy ranges between 15-20% of the overall portfolio.
  • QQA writes calls and puts for the income portion of the portfolio against Nasdaq futures and has a lower exposure at around 5-10% weight.
  • JEPQ has a TTM dividend of 9.51%
  • QQA is expected to have a forward dividend yield of around 10% according to Invesco and Bank of America Global Research.
  • Both payout the dividends and premiums as the total payout representative of the dividend paid by the fund.
  • JEPQ has an expense ratio of .35%
  • QQA has an expense ratio of .29% with a current special promotion: "Effective July 17, 2024 through December 31, 2024, Invesco Capital Management LLC (the “Adviser”) will voluntarily waive 100% of its management fee, 0.29%, for the Fund. The Net Expense Ratio for the Fund through December 31, 2024 is 0.00%."

Similarities

  • Both are run by very large institutions that have well-established derivatives trading desks.
  • Both utilize the ELN format for the options slice of the portfolio that provides income.
  • Both pay monthly dividends.

Why ELNs matter

This is the portion of the equation that had me up at night contemplating how it all worked. I had read the prospectus of both and listened to various interviews with Hamilton Reiner, but I still couldn't quite figure out all the moving parts.

After having a lengthy call with Invesco and chatting with JPMorgan a few times, I'm confident I have the crux of the equation, and it makes enough sense to me to be a viable strategy:

So, in order to avoid risking any of the actual equities in the portfolio being called away, the strategies simply take a proportion of the funds and fully fund their index futures option position with a counterparty. These funds are now encapsulated in note form, and any income from the buying or selling of options with the counterparty will be treated as income versus a return of capital.

From my understanding, these ELNs both buy an index future from an established counterparty and then write a call option against them [in most instances]. This is usually done monthly to match the monthly income. The counterparty would make some money on the spread for the sale of the index future, and both the buy/writer and the counterparty get a benefit.

Eventually, this fully funded position of trading with the counterparty would mature or end, after which I assume the funds are then reinvested into a similar setup again and again. The strategy would be ratcheted up or down depending on where the portfolio manager sees the options market going.

Why big institutions matter

There are many options overlay strategies out there, but only a select few can actually utilize the ELN format to where the manager does not have to risk the underlying stocks being called away. In many of the smaller option/stock ETFs we see in the market, NAV erosion is much more prevalent as actual stock positions are getting called away to provide the yield to the investor.

Oftentimes, the best options premiums are on the stocks with the highest momentum, and the manager who cannot utilize an ELN format may have to risk the stocks with the best momentum or RSI in order to keep up the yield.

Only institutions with established derivatives trading desks could easily set this up, and I would only recommend the funds that are run by large institutions currently for that reason.

Holdings comparisons

JEPQ Top 10

Ticker Security Description
(AAPL) APPLE INC COMMON STOCK
(NVDA) NVIDIA CORP COMMON STOCK
(MSFT) MICROSOFT CORP COMMON
(META) META PLATFORMS INC
(AMZN) AMAZON.COM INC COMMON
(GOOG) ALPHABET INC COMMON
(AVGO) BROADCOM INC COMMON
(TSLA) TESLA INC COMMON STOCK
(NFLX) NETFLIX INC COMMON STOCK
ELN NDX_19

As we can see with JEPQ, the top 10 holdings are a bit different than the Nasdaq 100 which we can see below with QQA and QQQ. With the higher weighting of options in the JEPQ portfolio, one of the ELN option notes also makes its way into the top 10 holdings.

This portfolio does have more active management and turnover than QQA which simply seeks to match the QQQ index fund.

QQA Top 10

Compared to QQQ

So as we can see, QQA is a true index fund replicator, as the top 10 of QQA are exactly the same as QQQ. There are some different holdings proportionality due to the cash and ELN positions, but the strategy is the same with no turnover unless the index changes.

Why monthly matters

My strategy with these funds is not to use the income as it rolls in, but rather reinvest it to smooth out the principal balance. As the funds hold stocks, they also move with the market, although their beta should be much lower than the market in general.

Monthly reinvestments will keep the return trajectory line smooth. Then depending on market conditions, bull or bear, I would simply sell LIFO [last in first out] or FIFO [first in first out]. In a bull, I would be selling last in first out as my older shares probably have more capital gains, and in the case of a bear market, sell first in first out assuming that the earliest sets of shares have some tax loss harvesting.

With monthly reinvesting, this selling of shares strategy should work out overall, as selecting individual share tranches by date usually has a tranche limitation. This is how I intend to operate this strategy for liquidity once I start moving some money market funds to utilize tax efficiency.

Examples of muted volatility

Here is a 3 portfolio example over the course of 1 year.

  • Portfolio 1 : JEPI 50% JEPQ 50%
  • Portfolio 2: JEPQ 100%
  • Portfolio 3: JEPI 100%

In this 1 year period, all three scenarios have a max drawdown that is around 50% less than the S&P 500 (SPY) or The Nasdaq (QQQ). During this same period of Jan 1 2023-Sep 30th 2024, the S&P 500 had a max drawdown of -8.32% and QQQ had a max drawdown of -8.42%.

Total returns all the while have been very comparable to their index targets. Furthermore, the monthly reinvesting versus quarterly quickly lowers your cost basis during drawdown periods.

The examples above are based on JPMorgan's products, but I expect the Invesco products to hold up as well.

Why own multiple?

My opinion is, owning a few different covered call overlay funds diversifies risk as it relates to the options trading strategies. As ELN agreements are bespoke to the negotiations between the two parties involved, these are not items that are disclosed and will perform better or worse depending on the talent of the manager.

Knowing how this works better, I now would only want to be involved in the index fund option overlay products that have institutions behind them that are large enough to execute the ELN structure. My hang-up on these was the NAV erosion possibility due to writing options against actual owned shares, which then creates further reinvestment risk after the manager gets back a chunk of cash for a stock that is now more expensive to go and repurchase in the market.

Other options

Other great products from the two companies that I would intend to cover later would be their more value-oriented products, namely The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and The Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Income Advantage ETF (RSPA). With JEPI more or less indexing the low-vol S&P 500 index and RSPA mimicking the S&P 500 equal eight, those are actually very attractive to me right now with lower overall P/E ratios for the underlying holdings.

Both of these again sell options within the ELN wrapper, which protects the stocks from being called away while still providing monthly income.

Risks

All of these strategies have risk associated with their options slice, which also entails counterparty risk. The main risks associated with this structure are laid out here by Invesco and should be consistent with all of the option overlay funds:

Investments in ELNs are susceptible to the risks of their underlying instruments, which could include management risk, market risk and, as applicable, foreign securities and currency risks. ELNs are also subject to certain debt securities risks, such as interest rate and credit risks. Should the prices of the underlying instruments move in an unexpected manner, the Fund may not achieve the anticipated benefits of an investment in an ELN, and may realize losses, which could be significant and could include the Fund’s entire principal investment. An ELN investment is also subject to counterparty risk, which is the risk that the issuer of the ELN will default or become bankrupt and the Fund may not be repaid the principal amount of, or income from, its investment. ELNs may also be less liquid than more traditional investments and the Fund may be unable to sell ELNs at a desirable time or price.

Again, this is limited to the slice that produces the income, and these funds further diversify the risk by splitting their ELN proportion amongst multiple ELNs with multiple counterparties.

Also, please note that some platforms may not allow you to trade ETFs with active management and or futures trading baked in. As of right now, Merrill Edge in particular does not allow investors to buy or sell some of these products.

Summary

For those who are looking to get more exposure to the market by redirecting capital into equities, these two funds provide a way to do that with lower volatility and not too much capped upside. Again, these products I think are excellent for a portion of your monthly expense accounts that you intend to liquidate to pay for day-to-day income needs. I still believe the majority of one's portfolio should be in long-term buy-and-hold stocks and index funds.

Both of these are run by large reputable firms that have advantages in writing options against smaller boutique firms. Both of these are a buy.

data.title: The Metals Company: High-Potential Deep-Sea Mining Company, But Regulatory And Financial Uncertainty Remain
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Tejo Strategy Investing as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis

The Metals Company (NASDAQ:TMC) is a Canada-based pre-revenue mining company which has incredible potential in deep-sea collecting of polymetallic nodules. These nodules contain high levels of manganese, nickel, copper and cobalt, making them highly desirable in a world transitioning to electrification and moving away from fossil fuels. The impact of this type of mining is limited; no infrastructure needs to be placed, no waste is made in the sea and limited waste offshore, no underground digging is needed.

TMC has been assigned different sectors of the Clarion-Clipperton Fracture Zone [CCFZ], which could account for roughly $500 billion in possible total turnover at current prices. NORI-D, the area that TMC plans to exploit first, could generate more than $8 billion of net present value and accounts for 22% of the total assigned seabed territory.

However, when the approvals will come is still unclear. TMC’s exploratory work has been finalized, and the company is now awaiting regulatory finalization to hopefully start collection at the beginning of 2026.

The electrification industry is currently still highly dependent on often highly polluting mining industries in other continents, some of which are banned from trade with the US or disincentivized, and fraught with longer-term geopolitical uncertainty.

Additionally, financing is uncertain. The company’s current cash is very low, but TMC states that financing is guaranteed by existing investors.

The company’s current valuation at about $330 million is low, even for standards of pre-revenue mining companies which are often valued on the basis of the asset value of the mine.

The Metals Company

Introduction

Let me start by focusing on the potential of TMC in a world focused on energy transition. Metals such as copper, nickel, cobalt or manganese, are at the heart of the global energy transition, and they are found most abundantly on the bottom of certain parts of the ocean. The demand for metals for the transition from fossil fuels to other energy sources is expected to increase 6x by 2040, and already at present the mining sector is facing difficulties coping with demand. The copper supply crisis is already here, due to increased demand and the lack of mine projects.

Typically, these minerals are mined on land, with difficult extraction processes, enormous CO2 emissions and environmental pollution. TMC’s goal is to collect polymetallic nodules off certain areas of the deep seabed. These nodules are composed of are the ones typically used in batteries used for the EV sector.

TMC’s goal is to collect polymetallic nodules, which are scattered abundantly on the seabed of the deep sea in the CCFZ. These nodules are located far offshore, at about 4 kilometers depth, with little sea life.

TMC’s collector picks up these nodules and transports them back to the ship for processing on land.

In March 2024, TMC announced the first-ever nickel sulfate ever generated by polymetallic nodules. In June 2024, a similar announcement was made for the first-ever cobalt sulfate generated by polymetallic nodules. In September 2024, TMC and PAMCO announced the successful production of 500 tonnes of calcine, which PAMCO intends to PAMCO smelt into a high-grade nickel-copper-cobalt alloy and manganese silicate. All three announcements are evidently key milestones.

TMC holds largest exploration areas

TMC company is furthest-advanced to starting deep sea collecting activities in certain allotments of the CCFZ between Hawaii and Mexico, the world’s largest estimated undeveloped source of critical battery metals.

TMC’s contract areas hold an estimated 15.9 million tons [Mt] of nickel, 2.2 Mt of cobalt, 355 Mt of manganese, and 13.3Mt of copper. That amount of said metals would also be enough to electrify the entire U.S. fleet, which, compared to China, has a fleet of electrical vehicles three times smaller. That makes TMC the holder of the second-largest nickel resource in the world, behind Russia’s extremely polluting Norilsk, which holds 18.9 Mt of nickel. In light of the U.S.’s target to reach 50% sales of electric vehicles by 2030, progress will need to happen fast here.

Nori-D is the furthest advanced of the zones awarded to TMC, has a current net present value of more than $8 billion, represents 41% of the NORI field and 22% of TMC’s total polymetallic nodule fields. Total gross revenue for only the NORI zone is estimated at about $100 billion, with nickel revenues accounting for about half of that revenue.

The above financial model would also imply that TMC could become the second lowest-cost nickel producer in the world.

Russia’s nornickel leads the way here, but the conditions of life and mining there are beyond imagination. Nornickel has left an entire geographical area severely polluted due to the lack of proper storage and spills.

ISA

However, regulatory uncertainty remains a concern. To start operations, TMC is dependent on action from the International Seabed Authority [ISA], an organization established in accordance with the 1994 UNCLOS agreement which has been empowered to award seabed exploration contracts. The vast majority of all countries in the entire world have ratified UNCLOS, though the U.S. has not, which is probably why TMC operates out of Canada. ISA has exclusively awarded TMC three of a total of 19 exploration contracts, 17 of which are in the CCZ, covering <4% of the global seafloor.

The big regulatory hurdle TMC has to take is one that fully depends on ISA, and TMC has had to repeatedly delay the expected start of commercialization as ISA is not moving forward fast enough. NORI, a TMC subsidiary, has filed an application to exploit minerals in 2021. That has triggered a two-year timeframe for ISA to complete regulations on deep sea mining. That two-year period in which ISA was supposed to provide a regulatory framework for deep sea collection of polymetallic nodules has meanwhile passed. In the March 2024 meeting, a finalization date of July 2025 was put forward.

That does not mean TMC cannot move forward, as the lack of regulations within two years of Nori’s application triggers the possibility for Nori to file an exploitation contract application. TMC assumes this should take a year to review.

TMC now plans to start production at the end of Q1 2026, assuming filing of an application for an exploitation contract at the beginning of 2025 and a one-year review process.

Production, processing and offtake readiness

TMC’s plan is to produce 1.3 million wet tons annually with one vessel at first, and to ramp up production to about 3 million wet tons annually over time. Tests with one ship have shown the ability to collect 14 tons of nodules per hour.

The processing of the nodules should eventually take place at Pamco in Japan, subject to some feasibility studies. The definitive contract with Pamco to that purpose is expected to be finalized by the end of 2024. Assuming approval to start exploitation in 2025, PAMCO could start processing the nodules as of 2026.

TMC has offtake agreements with Glencore in place giving Glencore the right to purchase up to 50% of the annual produced quantity originating from the NORI contract area, for the duration of exploitation of the NORI contract area. Glencore also made a $5 million equity investment into TMC.

Limited environmental concerns

I think the environmental concerns are all in all limited at this stage. Existing mining techniques have severe impacts on deforestation - often in rainforest areas - biodiversity, water use and pollution. Oil rigs have their own history of incidents and disasters.

Compared to existing mining techniques, I believe deep sea collection of polymetallic nodules is the road ahead, using a far better technique from the perspective of sustainability and environmental concerns. Additionally, the typical supply chain to bring these metals to the U.S. could be substantially reduced.

The dedicated vessel, Allseas’ The Hidden Gem which TMC has exclusive use of, currently has a collector on board that descends to the seabed supplied by electrical cables. The collector uses seawater jets aimed at nodules. Other than picking up the nodules, there is no excavation, cutting or drilling of sorts [Vimeo video].

The biggest environmental concern is the impact of the sediment plume generated by the seawater jets and collection. TMC’s ESIA program is intended to research the impact of the intended collection, and a collector test run in 2022 provided information on the impact of the intended collection activities. The test run’s preliminary conclusion as confirmed by other reports was that the seafloor sediment plume hugs the seafloor and is not transported long distances by ocean currents, essentially implying a rather small impact to the seabed. Sessile organisms identified during baseline studies were still present twelve months after nodule collection. The abyssal plain where operations would take place furthermore represents an area of the planet with the lowest biomass per square meter.

The impact on CO2 emissions would be drastic, too, compared to mining techniques currently used.

A potential shift away from geopolitical dependency

TMC's production should also allow the American continent to be less dependent on countries such as Russia and China, which may create a substantial political incentive. Dependency on mining areas in territories outside of Western territories or, worse, in countries who are no members of the Free Trade Agreement with the US, is a big issue in the global energy transition. It may not only raise production and cost issues, but also means a net outflow of monies to such countries. Over half of nickel production worldwide is located in Russia, China, Indonesia and the Philippines. TMC’s contract areas NORI and TOML account by far for the largest undeveloped nickel projects on the planet, and would rank second next to Russia’s nornickel if operations would begin, even if only NORI would be operated in the first instance.

In countries such as Democratic Republic of the Congo, where much of the world’s supply of copper and cobalt is mined, conditions are often not much better.

And probably for all metals related to the energy transition, which are all found in polymetallic nodules TMC would be collecting, the global energy transition will increase demand dramatically as shown in the slides below – examples focused on electric vehicles are given for nickel and manganese.

U.S. regulators seem to be realizing that they should not be left behind in all of this. Recently, about 350 government and military officials have urged the Senate to ratify the UNCLOS, which was hailed by TMC.

Valuation

Mining projects are often valued on the basis of their net present value.

In TMC’s case, with the total NPV for NORI-D estimated at $8.5 billion at current prices, the total TOML+NORI NPV would be about $40 billion at current prices. Total gross revenue for only the NORI zone is estimated at about $100 billion, with nickel revenues accounting for about half of that revenue. A rough extrapolation would mean total revenue for NORI and TOML could amount to about $500 billion, at current prices.

At a $330 million valuation, there is a huge discrepancy between the NPV for its TOML+NORI fields and even for merely the NORI-D field. Exploration of the Marawa field, a further exploration area that TMC is currently not focusing on, has not even been taken into account in that respect. No reasonable discount can justify such low valuation in my eyes, also taking into account that the regulatory or other uncertainty here is rather limited in my eyes.

Financials

Financing could be a concern if regulatory uncertainty remains for the years to come. TMC will at a given point need to raise capital, but prefers to do so by tapping into less dilutive means than adding shares, such as through credit facility announcements.

At the end of June 2024, TMC had $0.5 million in cash, down from $6.8 million at the end of 2023, and had short-term debt of $5.9 million. TMC has options to receive unsecured funding from each of its three largest shareholders. The company believes that its borrowing availability under a credit facility with an affiliate of Allseas, and its credit facility with ERAS Capital LLC and Mr. Barron will be sufficient to meet working capital and capital expenditure commitments for at least the next twelve months from today. There is a $25 million unsecured credit facility from the affiliate of Allseas and an unsecured $20 million credit facility provided by the largest shareholder Eras Capital and CEO Gerard Barron. Eras Capital is the family office of TMC director Andrei Karkar.

For the previous quarter, TMC reported a net loss of approximately $20.2 million. In comparison, net loss for the full year of 2023 was $73.8 million, of which $33.5 million was related to Q4 of 2023.

Risks

I believe a lot of the risk for TMC is regulatory and financial at this stage. No seafloor polymetallic nodule deposit has ever been commercially collected.

Critics of deep sea collection not only originate from the environmental side, but also from the financial side, considering that it’s financially risky.

Competition from China, Russia or Indonesia may be less burdened by regulatory constraints both for collection as for processing, and 16 other ISA member states or private companies sponsored by such states hold exploration contracts. That means that competition in the deep sea collection market may eventually become more fierce, and other big players such as DEME Group may eventually compete in the same field.

Seeking Alpha's Quant program mentions the stock is at risk of performing poorly.

Conclusion

The Metals Company holds large potential as a deep sea mining company, but at the same time regulatory and financial uncertainty remain.

Clearly, TMC could become a big player in the future of mining, as the world transitions into sustainable ways to manufacture batteries for renewable energy storage. Deep sea polymetallic nodules are scattered on the floor of the Clarion-Clipperton Fracture zone contain cobalt, nickel, copper and manganese, and all of these are highly desired for the global future energy needs.

The NORI-D field would be the first to exploit, and TMC has been a front-runner in trying to have the international regulatory authority ISA regulate exploitation and in assessing the impact of its potential future activities. TMC hopes to begin collection at the beginning of 2026.

Mining is a necessity in today’s world. TMC plans to do so in a sustainable way, with less impact on biodiversity than other mining activities and a high yield, much less emissions, and on a low cost-basis. The potential is huge, with 15.9 million tonnes of nickel alone making it the second biggest undeveloped nickel project globally. I believe the clock is ticking, not just for global warming, but also in light of the U.S.’s target to reach 50% sales of electric vehicles by 2030.

I estimated the total NPV of TMC’s TOML and NORI fields at about $40 billion, based on an NPV of $8.5 for the NORI-D field as calculated by TMC at current metal prices. TMC’s trades at a fraction of those NPV’s.

The only things preventing a bull case scenario for TMC at this point are regulatory uncertainty and the lack of cash. It is still unclear when ISA will give the green light. TMC keeps stating that it has sufficient funding through credit facilities, but it would be better in my eyes – given the high quarterly cash burn – to have additional cash on hand.

For the above reasons, TMC is a Hold for me at this stage.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Travelers Companies: Great Numbers, But Can It Keep The Momentum?
data.description:
Introduction

The Travelers Companies, Inc. (NYSE:TRV) came out with a blowout Q3 earnings report that comfortably beat consensus in every aspect. I wanted to go through some numbers and give some comments on performance and what I think about the company and the insurance business in general. It's a tough industry to evaluate as it can get quite complex, but I thought to initiate my coverage with TRV and see how it has been performing.

In my opinion, the reason the company performed so well was because of easy comps from last year. However, if the company can maintain this momentum and continues to reward its investors through dividend growth and consistent buybacks, the share price should continue to climb.

However, I am going to sit on the fence for now and will continue to evaluate the company and insurance business overall before committing any capital.

Q3 Results

Net written premiums were $11.317B, up 8% year over year, as the company saw growth in all three reported segments. Net income was $1.26B, with core income constituting the bulk, $1.218B. The underwriting profit margin came in at around 13.25%, with underlying underwriting income up 73% y/y, at $1.5B.

On a per-share basis, the company saw a whopping 211% increase in net income per diluted share to $5.42, while core income per diluted share was up 169% at $5.24. Book value per share was up 39% y/y to $122, while adjusted book value per share was up 13% to $131.30.

One of the main metrics for insurance companies, the return on equity, came in at 19.2%, with core ROE at 16.6%. ROE last year was 7.7%. The company turned to underwriting profits compared to last year, with a combined ratio coming in at 93.2%, or 7.8 points lower than last year. If we strip out the factors that distort the company's underwriting profitability, like the catastrophe losses, favorable development on prior-year losses, and certain investment gains/losses, the company's underlying combined ratio came in at an excellent 85.6%. This was a whopping 5% improvement on last year.

These results seem like night and day compared to the same period last year. It could be said that because the results last year were terrible, an easy comp is not surprising, but the company has delivered above and beyond with growth and efficiency improvements across the board.

Comments on the Performance

The company's main financial metrics are heading in the right direction. With book value per share coming in at $122, the company's P/B sits at around 2.15. Some people may think this is a little high, but if we look at the competition, as provided by default by Seeking Alpha, TRV's P/B ratio is somewhere close to the lowest. This means the company is not as expensive as the other players in the sector. The company is more than capable of increasing its book value considerably y/y, so this is rewarded by investors, and a P/B ratio of 2.2 reflects the company's ability to compound book value per share.

In terms of return on equity, the company is close to what I would consider quite profitable, especially after this recent report. The company's ROE came in just under what I would like to see, which is 20%. However, when we look at the same period last year, we can see a tremendous improvement. I would like to see the company maintain this sort of profitability over the long run. Furthermore, compared to the peers, and I believe SA's figures are not yet updated with Q3 results, TRV should rank a little higher than where it is shown in the image below, considering the recent improvements y/y.

I am also very impressed with how much the company's combined ratio has improved from just a year ago. If you are new to insurance companies (which I am too), the lower the combined ratio, the more profitable the insurance company is. What that measures is the underwriting expenses and loss payments relative to total earned premiums, so if expenses total less than what the company managed to earn in premiums, that means the company is profitable. Last year, the company was above 100%, which meant it was making an underwriting loss. An ideal underwriting profit margin is somewhere between 10% and 15%, and this quarter, the company was bang in the middle of it, which tells me that the company is very efficient at making a profit.

Another important metric to look at is how the company uses the float available, which is the difference between the premiums received and claims paid out. This is cash that is not the insurance company's but is invested to generate income. This was 16% higher than last year, driven by what looks like a reliable return on the company's growing fixed-income portfolio, which makes up most of the portfolio, and equity securities performed well during the same period. Of course, an insurance company cannot play around with other people's money and invest in risky assets. That's why equity securities sit at $652m in fair value, while fixed income is at around $84B, with quite a bit of unrealized losses during the period. Nevertheless, the company consistently bags around 3% from these investments, which is decent and risk-free.

Now that the Fed has begun cutting interest rates, it will be interesting to see how this will affect the company's fixed-income portfolio. Usually, as interest rates fall, the value of existing government bonds increases. Those unrealized losses that we see in the company's 10-Q should start to reverse, but I expect lower quarterly income going forward since because of these lower interest rates.

Comments on the Outlook

About half of the company's catastrophe losses came in from Hurricane Helene, which impacted the company financially in Georgia and the Carolinas regions the most, while the company did not experience much financial burden in Florida. Hurricane Helene made landfall just north-west of Florida in the Big Bend coastal region, which has relatively low population density, so it makes sense that the company did not suffer much financial burden in the region; however, Hurricane Milton made landfall in Florida's central west coast as a category three hurricane. On the Q3 earnings call, the management said it is still early to report the costs of Milton, but so far, the damages stand between $75m and $175m pre-tax. Looking at the trajectories of the two hurricanes, I think Helene will cost a lot more than Milton since it hit a lot more states, whereas Milton mostly hit Florida. I know some people who traveled to South Carolina during the hurricane, and for the most part, South Carolina was fine. Thus, I am expecting much fewer catastrophe losses for TRV when they report it next quarter - unless we get another hurricane.

Since this quarter was a blowout, I would like to see how the next quarter compares. I would like to see the company maintain decent top-line growth of around 8% while increasing its ROE and decreasing its combined ratio further to solidify itself as a great company that is capable of generating above-average profits.

Am I going to Invest in TRV?

In my view, the quarter was excellent mainly because the same quarter last year was terrible. I am not expecting the next quarter to be that positive for The Travelers Companies, Inc. Over 200% y/y growth in net income per share isn't difficult to achieve when the comps are easy. I would like to see how these numbers compare sequentially, and after the easy comps are out of the way. Can the company sustain its mid-80s underlying combined ratio going forward and average over 20% ROE? It is too early to tell still, and one great quarter doesn't tell me a lot. So, I would like to see the company maintaining this momentum going forward.

TRV has also been generous in rewarding its shareholders via share buybacks and dividends, which have been very steady. The dividend yield is still quite low, which, to me, just says that there is a lot of room for growth in the future. The company has grown its dividend for 18 years straight, so if the dividend is what you invest for primarily, and you have a long horizon ahead, it may be a decent, safe bet.

Now, in terms of buybacks, the company repurchased 1.1m shares this quarter and is on track to repurchase around 4m by the end of the year. That means the company purchases less than 2% a year, which I don't think is too bad. The company consistently repurchased shares for the last decade and reduced its share count by almost 33%. Consistent dividend growth and share repurchases tell me that the management is confident in the business and wants to reward shareholders.

However, I would like to do a lot more research on the insurance business and the company itself before committing any capital. I wanted to share my thoughts on the company and hear your thoughts on the subject and what you think about the company's efforts in rewarding its shareholders and overall operations.

data.title: Benitec Biopharma: OPMD Program Continues To Advance With Catalysts
data.description:
Benitec Biopharma Inc. (NASDAQ:BNTC) recently reported positive results from its phase 1b/2a clinical study using BB-301 for the treatment of patients with Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy [OPMD]. It reported these positive interim results at two time points during 2024 showing initial proof-of-concept that this gene therapy works in being able to treat these patients with this rare disorder.

Why should investors care about this program though now that these catalysts have come and gone? It is because this positive data allows the company to move the 3rd patient into the same dosing regimen in October 2024 for starters. Secondly, it allows it to move to higher dosing of BB-301 in 2025, which may ultimately yield an improvement in efficacy.

Speaking of which, it is highly likely that there should be the release of data from such higher dosing during this period as well. If all goes smoothly with this program regarding low and high-dosing, then it will be in good shape to initiate a late-stage study for this program in 2026. With the ability to advance BB-301 as a gene therapy treatment option for patients with OPMD, plus several catalysts on the way within the next few years, I believe that investors can capitalize on any potential gains made here.

BB-301 For The Treatment Of Patients With OPMD

As I noted above in the beginning, the main clinical program from this company would be the development of gene therapy BB-301 for the treatment of patients with Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy. The use of this therapy is being explored in the ongoing phase 1b/2a study. Before going over this specific trial, plus any catalysts to come out of this program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for it could be.

Ocularpharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy [OPMD] is a rare genetic disorder that leads to muscle weakness. Having said that, it is a type of muscular dystrophy. However, OPMD only affects the muscles of the throat and eyelids. This is a hard disorder to detect because someone who has it doesn't likely get diagnosed with it until they are 40 years of age. The thing to note is that it is a progressive type of disorder that just continues to get worse over time. However, while it does progress in such a fashion, it does so at a slow pace.

Some symptoms that these patients with OPMD experience are as follows:

  • Unable to speak
  • Double vision
  • Facial weakness
  • Impaired eye movement
  • Weakness of the tongue
  • Dysphagia [Difficulty in swallowing].

The global Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy market size is expected to surpass $10 billion by the end of 2036. This is a good market size and the hope is that this company can continue to advance this program. What exactly causes this disorder? It is caused by a mutation of the PABPN1 gene, which is inherited either by one or both parents. Even though one can have it in either of these scenarios, patients who obtain two copies of mutated PABN1 gene develop more severe symptoms earlier in the process.

To see if BB-301 can help treat OPMD patients, it is running the phase 1b/2a clinical study. This particular trial has recruited a total of 2 patients thus far and then a 3rd patient is expected to be enrolled during the month of October 2024. There was positive interim data analysis from 1 patient reported at the 90-day and 180-day time points. The improvements observed as this one patient received one dose of BB-301 is as follows:

  • 90-day post treatment period — improvement in swallowing function
  • 180-day post treatment period — TPR was meaningfully reduced [in essence, smaller amounts of food in the pharynx for patients taking the gene therapy compared to an OPMD natural history study].

In terms of safety, BB-301 was shown to be very safe. Matter of fact, the only adverse event [AE] noted in this particular trial from either of these two patients was a Grade 2 Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease [GERD]. Even better than this, there were no serious adverse events [SAEs] reported from either of these two particular patients enrolled. The third patient remains an unknown for now. Thus, it is too early to say whether there will be any safety issues to be released later on.

Again, even though these set of results were already released from this ongoing phase 1b/2a clinical study, there are still a few other catalysts that investors can consider. Some of which may or may not ultimately yield a higher stock price. The first of which is that there will be the release of updated results, from this mid-stage trial using higher dosing of BB-301 for OPMD patients, in 2025. The reason this is possible is that the company is going to use higher dosing of BB-301 for the treatment of these patients during this period. From there, if everything goes smoothly, then it is expected that Benitec will be in a position to initiate a late-stage pivotal study for this particular program.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Benitec Biopharma had cash and cash equivalents of $50.9 million as of June 30th of 2024. The reason for the cash on hand is that it did a PIPE financing agreement back in April 2024. This was an oversubscribed private placement financing agreement of $40 million done on April 22nd of 2024. This was a crucial financing event, especially considering that it greatly extended its cash runway. It now believes that it has a cash runway, or enough cash on hand to fund its operations, through 2025. It has a cash burn $11.3 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into the spending of $7.8 million in R&D expenses and then $3.5 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Benitec Biopharma. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the higher dosing of BB-301 for the treatment of patients with OPMD. The goal is to test out higher doses of this gene therapy in 2025 to see if efficacy can be improved upon. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that moving to a higher dose of BB-301 will result in an improved clinical outcome.

The second risk is in terms of safety because there is no assurance that the third patient enrolled in the lower dosing of BB-301 will not experience any type of SAE. In addition, there is no way of knowing whether the higher dosing will have safety issues upon trial completion. If any safety issues are found in the higher dosing of BB-301, then that would force the company into a strategy of only moving to a pivotal study with a lower dose.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of advancing BB-301 for the treatment of patients with OPMD on the regulatory front. It is the intention of the company to move the development of this program forward towards pivotal phase 3 testing. Despite obtaining positive data from the ongoing phase 1b/2a study, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to allow it to move towards initiating such a trial. Nor, that the final results to be released from such a late-stage study will be positive.

Conclusion

Benitec Biopharma has done well to continue to advance its lead gene therapy BB-301 for the treatment of patients with OPMD. That's because it already reported positive interim analysis from one patient at day 90 and 180 from the phase 1b/2a study using this gene therapy to treat these patients. Not only that, but it posted positive interim data again afterward, showing that two patients had achieved improvement in terms of swallow function. This was the first gene therapy of its kind to help OPMD patients regarding this improvement.

The premise now is that the company still has plenty of catalysts on the way. In 2025, there will be the recruitment of higher dosing of BB-301 for OPMD. Plus, likely data to be released occasionally during the same year. As long as this program runs smoothly with higher dosing, then the company should be on track to get a pivotal phase 3 study going in 2026.

data.title: Annovis Bio To Continue Alzheimer's Disease Clinical Trials
data.description:
Introduction

Annovis Bio is a small biotech company focused on neurodegenerative disorders. Initially, we anticipated covering this stock three times—prior to Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) and Parkinson’s Disease (PD) data, post-AD data, and post-PD data. However, this article marks the third follow-up to our original article on Annovis from January 2024, and the stock is down approximately 40% since then. This update will be brief and will provide an overview of the facts, as we see them, since our initial coverage, particularly regarding the development of Buntanetap in AD. We recommend that readers familiarize themselves with the entirety of our prior due diligence (Article 1, Article 2, Article 3), with special emphasis on the coverage of phase 2/3 data in AD (Article 2).

What happened on April 29th, 2024?

As per our previous coverage, on April 29th of this year, Annovis announced topline data from the phase 2/3 Alzheimer's Disease (AD) trial through a press release, which resulted in the stock plummeting by 70%. The data presented in the press release focused on post-hoc subset populations, and, as we initially noted, appeared unrelated to the intention-to-treat population. Analyzing post-hoc data in AD trials is a well-known tactic to show some signs of efficacy. However, to anyone with a basic understanding of statistics and study design, such maneuvers are easy to spot and dispute. When an undisclosed number of comparisons are performed on a given data set, it is expected that some statistically significant findings will emerge—these are, in fact, (most likely) false positive results. The probability of at least one false positive result increases with the number of comparisons, which can be plotted to illustrate this phenomenon (Smith et al., 2021).

We noted how Annovis analyzed data according to a seemingly arbitrary choice of biomarkers and baseline thresholds for MMSE scores. Furthermore, there were inconsistencies related to the dose-response relationships in the changes of biomarkers reported by the sponsor.

Overall, we concluded that the phase 2/3 trial of Buntanetap was a failure, as the drug did not demonstrate the anticipated efficacy. Our assessment has since proven accurate—the drug, it appears, shows no cognitive benefit, regardless of the dose administered.

In the primary analysis of the ITT population (which represents the formal primary endpoint and the most important outcome), Buntanetap failed to show any benefit. Every group improved, regardless of whether patients were taking a placebo or Buntanetap. In fact, the placebo group numerically outperformed the lowest dose arm. There was no statistically significant difference between any of the treatment arms and the placebo arm.

What now?

One might expect that following a trial that was, in our view, a complete failure in demonstrating efficacy, the drug would be shelved, and further trials would be discontinued. This would likely be the course of action for a large biopharma company, which could afford to absorb such losses and pivot its strategy. However, for a small biotech like Annovis, Buntanetap is its be-all and end-all. The company has no other drugs in late-stage development and is considerably short on capital—this project has to work.

On October 15th, Annovis announced via a press release that "the FDA granted clearance to proceed with pivotal phase 3 studies, based on the Company’s Phase 2/3 clinical data showing improvement in early AD patients." The question is, what improvement is the sponsor referring to? The phase 2/3 trial reported no improvement in the pre-specified analysis, and the perceived "improvement" came from flawed, post-hoc comparisons.

The company is now claiming that a successful 6-month phase 3 trial "may be sufficient to support an NDA filing," effectively counting their chickens before they hatch. To say that these chickens are unlikely to hatch would be an understatement.

Annovis also reports that the FDA raised no concerns over the drug’s safety—which is unsurprising, given that the trials never reported significant safety issues. Considering that the drug had no effect on cognition in the ITT population and was not associated with adverse events, we believe that the most plausible explanation is that the drug is inert. To remind the reader, Buntanetap is a small molecule drug with a molar mass of approximately 337 g/mol.

Considering the available clinical data, arguing that this compound has demonstrated any convincing efficacy or could meaningfully impact AD pathophysiology appears rather outlandish. It draws parallels to another drug currently in development by Cassava Sciences—Simufilam. While not the primary focus of this article, like Buntanetap, Simufilam is a small molecule drug (259 g/mol) with a simple structure.

Similarly to Annovis' drug, Simufilam is proclaimed as potentially "disease modifying", despite that the alleged clinical "benefit" comes from open label trials with historical comparisons and non-statistically significant findings in the blinded randomized parts of the proof-of-concept trials. Fortunately, or unfortunately, unlike Buntanetap, the public will know whether Simufilam is efficacious soon—allegedly by the end of 2024 (ReTHINK topline data). In the case of Buntanetap, it appears we will have to wait for another AD trial to complete, which will likely take over a year, accounting for the enrollment period and the six-month treatment duration, to find out whether it can show efficacy in a phase 3 study.

Who is going to pay for it?

Running another phase 3 trial (or potentially a series of phase 3 trials) is not cheap. Annovis has been struggling with capital for a while now, reporting cash on hand of approximately $4M at the end of Q2 2024 and an operating loss of almost $8M. Judging by the statement of cash flows from the last three quarters, the company has mostly been financing its operations through proceeds from the issuance of common stock or warrants. Unless Annovis can convince someone to provide a sizable amount of capital to support their operations, running even one phase 3 trial might prove challenging. The current market capitalization is $100M, so it seems implausible that the company could raise enough money through at-the-market stock sales to finance the announced studies.

Conclusion

Our stance on Annovis remains exactly the same as it was at the beginning of the year. The lead asset—Buntanetap—is not a promising agent for either AD or PD. The company is low on cash and must secure financing if they want to even consider running a phase 3 clinical trial.

Hence, we believe that Annovis Bio is a sell.

data.title: DINT: Overexposure To China Secured Recent Momentum, Detracted From Long-Term Returns
data.description:
The core principle of momentum investing dictates that if an asset price appreciates steadily, it is in an investor's best interest to buy into it as soon as possible, as it will continue moving higher for some time. This is a very powerful yet extremely risky way of reasoning. I have never spoken openly against utilizing the momentum factor in order to make an equity portfolio weightier fast, but I have been repeating time and again that becoming too focused on that can cost you dearly.

Today's article is supposed to examine the Davis Select International ETF (BATS:DINT), a vehicle with a high-conviction ex-U.S. strategy favoring compactness over breadth of exposure. At first glance, this is not a momentum-focused ETF, but this impression can change quickly after looking at its one-month and YTD returns that have contributed to the A+ Momentum Quant grade as of October 17.

And on top of that, DINT has trounced the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) with ease this year.

This begs a fair question: why not buy into an ETF that is capable of outperforming the U.S. market even without having exposure to U.S. stocks at all (DINT is focused on EM and DM equities and ignores the U.S. intentionally)? The Fed is cutting interest rates, and that should put some pressure on the dollar, allowing ex-U.S. portfolios to take the lead. At the same time, valuations are ballooning as tech and tech-ish plays climb higher and higher, with the weighted-average market cap of the S&P 500 now hovering above $1 trillion, so gaining exposure to global stocks, that for the most part do not trade with the premium U.S. names command, looks rational and potentially rather lucrative.

Nevertheless, I would argue against it. Today, I would like to focus on the bigger story surrounding DINT's recent outperformance and also offer a closer look at other issues that render creating a Buy thesis impossible. But first, let us quickly discuss the basics of its strategy.

DINT strategy: pursuing winners outside the U.S.

DINT's strategy is mostly similar to that of the Davis Select U.S. Equity ETF (DUSA), a fund I cover regularly, with the key differentiator being its international ex-U.S. focus. At the same time, according to its summary prospectus that can be found on its website, DUSA "may invest up to 20% of net assets in non-U.S. companies." However, at this juncture, DUSA's exposure to non-U.S. companies is minimal (for example, via Toronto-quoted Tourmaline Oil (OTCPK:TRMLF), which accounts for just around 2% of its net assets).

As described in the most recent factsheet:

Dint is an actively managed portfolio of attractive businesses principally outside the U.S. selected using the time-tested Davis Investment Discipline. The Fund’s investment objective is long-term growth of capital.

Incepted in March 2018, the ETF has amassed $204.6 million in AUM. Its expense ratio is 66 bps. This might look justified considering the complex high-conviction strategy. However, there are two nuances here. First, it did detract significantly from its total returns in the past. Second, performance (including risk considerations) overall leaves a lot to be desired, so such a fee here looks too burdensome.

DINT portfolio: meaningful China exposure means elevated risks

As of October 15, DINT's portfolio consisted of 26 equities, with the top holding being Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF), a Hong Kong-quoted (HK ticker 3690) Chinese tech retail heavyweight with 8.8% weight in the portfolio. Overall, Chinese names account for 28.6% of the net assets, and they were mostly responsible for DINT's outstanding performance this year. The iShares MSCI China ETF (MCHI) adds the necessary context.

Speaking about the reasons why Chinese equities were in the limelight in September, the top contributor was the announcement of stimulus measures that were designed to reinvigorate the country's economy. However, as we can see from the October performance, the impetus has already waned.

Also, as I will illustrate below, substantial exposure to China likely resulted in its less appealing long-term returns.

Overall, DINT has exposure to 15 countries, including 11 developed and three emerging. One country (Bermuda) does not have a classification.

At the same time, the iShares MSCI ACWI ex U.S. ETF (ACWX) is much lighter in Chinese equities, with more emphasis put on developed markets like Japan and the UK.

Next, in terms of sectors, DINT favors consumer discretionary and financials. It completely ignores consumer staples, health care, and utilities.

Also, it is worth noting that I was surprised to find Danske Bank A/S (DNKEY) (Copenhagen ticker DANSKE) in this basket (6.2% weight) as DUSA removed this stock at some point between October 2, 2023, and January 4, 2024, which I have discussed in my article earlier this year. I am of course not aware of the exact reason why Danske was removed from DUSA's portfolio, but I surmise the decision was influenced by its valuation exiting the target range. However, surprisingly, it is still considered a promising pick by DINT.

DINT performance: material issues on the risk front

I started today's article by stating that DINT has been phenomenally successful recently. However, is the long-term picture similarly rosy? Alas, multiple issues arise here.

Since DINT is benchmarked against the MSCI ACWI ex US index, it seems totally logical to compare its performance to that of ACWX. I would also like to add the Vanguard Total International Stock ETF (VXUS) to the group, with the reason for that being its ex-U.S. focus. Also, it is of interest whether DINT was capable of beating the U.S. market or at least offering a more comfortable risk profile, since its more comfortable valuation could have been supportive of that. To assess that, I added IVV.

Metric DINT IVV ACWX VXUS
Start Balance $10,000 $10,000 $10,000 $10,000
End Balance $13,183 $24,310 $13,736 $13,900
CAGR 4.34% 14.64% 5.01% 5.20%
Standard Deviation 21.68% 17.38% 16.54% 16.80%
Best Year 29.60% 31.25% 21.03% 21.75%
Worst Year -22.90% -18.16% -16.04% -16.09%
Maximum Drawdown -39.67% -23.93% -28.31% -27.76%
Sharpe Ratio 0.2 0.75 0.24 0.25
Sortino Ratio 0.3 1.14 0.35 0.36
Benchmark Correlation 0.84 0.87 1 1
Upside Capture 110.46% 115.32% 100% 101.23%
Downside Capture 112.78% 75.62% 100% 100.41%
Expense Ratio (as of October 16) 0.66% 0.03% 0.34% 0.08%

Data from Portfolio Visualizer. The period is April 2018-September 2024. ACWX was used as a benchmark

The takeaways are as follows:

  • DINT underperformed all other selected funds, with one of the main reasons, as I can assume, being its burdensome expense ratio.
  • Its risk metrics were also far from appealing, as it had the highest volatility over the period, and risk-adjusted returns reflect that.
  • Its maximum drawdown of almost 40% (delivered in October 2022) was meaningfully deeper than those of the selected funds. The downside capture ratio is also a disappointment.
  • The upside capture ratio is a bright spot, though.
  • And on a side note, it is interesting to see that IVV captured just 75.6% of the international equities' downside and more than 115% of their upside.

Then, I decided to delve a bit deeper to investigate whether certain periods and factors had an outsized effect on the results above. Upon more attentive inspection, I found out that DINT beat ACWX in four out of the seven calendar years it has in the books (including 2018 and 2024). This year (January-September), it has been leading by a massive 15.8%. Another blockbuster year was 2020, when it beat the iShares MSCI ACWI ex U.S. ETF by 12.5%. So why does its long-term annualized return look so bleak? In July 2021, the worst month in its history so far, DINT lost 13.27% when China-light ACWX was down by only 1.65%. As a reminder, back then, Chinese stocks were put through the wringer amid the regulatory crackdown. I used the Wayback Machine to check whether DINT was overweight in Chinese equities back then. My hypothesis was confirmed: it had 30.8% allocated to them as of July 30, 2021. The takeaway here is simple: the exact same factor that has supercharged DINT's gains earlier this year can quickly wipe out a solid share of returns.

Final thoughts

DINT does an excellent job pairing the stability of developed markets with the risk/reward profile of their emerging counterparts. However, this mastery did not translate into consistently solid returns in the past, as it substantially underperformed ACWX, mostly owing to over-reliance on Chinese stocks. The risk profile of this ETF is anything but appealing to me, especially its downside capture ratio. And while I appreciate its recent momentum driven by the ebullience of the Chinese equity market, I think investors should not focus too much on it. Instead, it is worth paying more attention to historical results that were far from stellar. All in all, I believe it does not deserve a Buy rating.

data.title: Wolfspeed: Set Up For Big Future
data.description:
Wolfspeed, Inc. (NYSE:WOLF) has crashed since June due to fears of the capital needed to reach full production targets to meet huge design-in wins. The company just announced major funding from the government that, along with an investment from Apollo, sets up the capital needed to reach big goals. My investment thesis remains ultra-Bullish on the stock, still trading far below previous levels and at a deep discount.

Capital Raise

The silicon carbide company is soaring on news of funding to complete their massive manufacturing buildout to provide domestic production of silicon carbide to power clean energy systems. In total, Wolfspeed got $2.5 billion in funding as follows:

  • U.S. Department of Commerce proposes $750 Million in CHIPS and Science Act funding to support Wolfspeed’s North Carolina expansion and catalyze New York expansion
  • Company secures an additional $750 Million in financing from an investment group led by Apollo, The Baupost Group, Fidelity Management & Research Company and the Capital Group
  • Company also expects to receive approximately $1 Billion in Section 48D cash tax refunds from IRS over the coming years

A big key here is that Wolfspeed is only taking on $750 million in debt to obtain the $2.5 billion in capital. The company ended the June quarter with capital plans as follows.

Wolfspeed had already forecast a large reduction in capex spending in the current FY25. The company had plans for spending $1.2 to $1.4 billion in capex, followed by only $0.2 to $0.6 billion in FY26, depending on access to capital and orders.

The company ended FY25 with a large cash balance of $2.1 billion and debt of $6.1 billion. Wolfspeed is now that clear the company has the funding to complete the JP facility in Siler City.

Big Future

Wolfspeed now has the capital to compete the facilities for a revenue run rate in a few years reaching $3 billion. The Siler City facility only starts qualifying crystals and delivering wafers by summer 2025, so the full ramp up of facilities won't take place until 2026, or FY27.

As long detailed, Wolfspeed already has the design-ins to easily reach $3 billion in annual sales. The company has now reached $27.2 billion in cumulative power device design-ins by adding up to $2.0 billion of design-ins on a quarterly basis last FY.

Wolfspeed turned those designs into $0.5 billion of design-wins in the last quarter alone. These wins support 125 different car models across more than 30 OEMs with 80% of the orders tied to EVs and will deliver revenues peaking in about 4 years.

The market should start to lose the fears of a capital crunch preventing Wolfspeed from reaching full potential. Now the question will move to revenues finally showing those benefits.

The Mohawk Valley Fab has finally reached 25% utilization, with a target for just $50 to $60 million revenue contribution in FQ1 '25. The facility will eventually reach $200 million in quarterly revenues alone, but the ramp has been very slow.

The current consensus analyst estimates aren't buying Wolfspeed can turn the big design-ins into $3 billion in annual revenues anytime soon. On the Investor Update call, management confirmed the target is just a few years away, while analysts have the company taking until FY30 to reach this target.

Wolfspeed only has a market cap of $2 billion, while the stock traded double the current price just in June. Not to mention, Wolfspeed was a $45+ stock at the end of 2023 suggesting investors are willing to award a much higher valuation to the stock under normal market conditions.

A peer like Coherent (COHR) moving into the 200mm SiC sector trades at 3x sales. Wolfspeed would need a $9 billion market cap in a few years to match this valuation level, a level above where the stock has traded this year.

Naturally, the government has only given a loan commitment, so Wolfspeed doesn't have the money yet. The company faces 200mm SiC competitors from China that could impact sales towards the end of the decade.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Wolfspeed, Inc. now appears fully funded for a strong future. The market can now shift its focus towards revenue growth, and the company will hopefully provide some sales boosts in the quarters ahead as the Mohawk facility ramps.

The stock has rallied off recently lows around $8, but Wolfspeed is still very beaten down for the opportunity ahead.

data.title: TSMC: Shrinking Nanometers, Growing Top Line - Buy
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) is among our top three picks in our semi-coverage universe, and Thursday morning's Q3 earnings results and outlook confirm our positive thesis is well-placed for next year. TSMC reported a 54% Y/Y and 31.2% Q/Q surge in net profit for Q3, with net income hitting 325.3B Taiwanese dollars (about $10.1B) for the July-September quarter, comfortably surpassing the consensus of $300.2B Taiwanese dollars ($9.35B). The results pushed the stock over 12% higher Thursday so far. Net revenue increased 36% Y/Y and 12.9% Q/Q to $23.50B on a gross margin of 57.8%, up 4.6% Q/Q and 39% Y/Y from 54.3% in a year ago quarter and 53.2% last quarter.

TSMC stock is up +112% since our buy-rating, a little over a year ago, in October of last year, versus the S&P 500, up 33% during the same period, supported by our investment thesis stating:

"We think the weakness is priced in and see end demand recovery in +44% of TSMC's end market exposure happening in 2024, and longer-term growth tailwinds from A.I."

TSMC's outperformance in FY24 has been largely supported by AI tailwinds as end demand outside of AI remains lacking; ASML confirmed this chip weakness earlier this week with their bookings miss and reduced guidance range for FY25 bookings. TSMC management notes in the Q3 revenue analysis that outperformance was driven by "strong smartphone and AI-related demand for industry-leading 3nm and 5nm technologies," with 3nm process tech making up 20% of total wafer revenue for the quarter while 5nm and 7nm represented 32% and 17%, respectively.

The industry is moving to smaller nanometer process tech for AI to keep up with Moore's Law, which should benefit TSMC, in our opinion, as TSMC dominates the global foundry market with a 62% share as of 2QCY24. TSMC's top customers, Apple, Nvidia, Qualcomm, and AMD, are showing strong demand "nearly fully booking TSMC's 3nm capacity." Now, we expect TSMC's next leg of outperformance to be supported once again by AI, as well as smartphones, on the specific thesis that both moving to smaller nanometer nodes will work in TSMC's favor. We emphasize to investors that while some positives have been priced in after Thursday's report, we still see more room for upside surprise next year.

Nanometer Size Matters

The semiconductor industry can never remain static; there is a constant strive to achieve Moore's Law from Intel co-founder Gordon Moore, which states "the number of transistors on an integrated circuit will double every two years with minimal rise in cost." That's what customers are chasing when moving to smaller nanometer that'll allow more transistors to "be squeezed into the same area."

AI is currently at 4 nm with Nvidia Blackwell GPUs using TSMC's 4nm process, but quickly, AI will have to move to 3nm and then 2nm processes, which will be through TSMC as the industry-go-to with Nvidia and Apple as its two largest customers. TrendForce report about the strong momentum in 3nm demand noted, "Following the widespread adoption of TSMC's 3nm process, the 3nm supply chain has attracted significant market attention, with expectations for operational boosts in the coming year." We expect AI moving to small nanometers will result in a higher 3nm revenue by technology, as 3nm and 2nm come with a higher price tag. Analyst Tom O'Malley noted that 2nm will likely get a "bigger initial revenue contribution than N3 [3nm]." We expect top-line growth supported by this industry transition from AI to smaller nodes, as well as the smartphone transition. Apple's iPhone 16 uses TSMC's 3nm, and we should also see a transition to 2nm there down the line.

The following charts showcase TSMC's revenue by technology for 3Q24.

We're also more positive about TSMC's position to lead the industry transition to smaller nanometer nodes. The expectations are for capex this year to pick up to over $30B, with this quarter's capex higher at $6.4B versus an average of $6.36B for the first three-quarters of FY24. Management expects capital spend for the current quarter will double to approximately $11.5B due to healthy demand, with CEO C.C. Wei noting on the earnings call that "the demand is real." TSMC is also keeping up with the U.S. interest to move chip making to U.S. soil, investing $65B in three plants in Arizona, with the first fab estimated to see volume production in 2025. The graph below outlines TSMC's capital expenditure since 1Q22.

Valuation & Word on Wall Street

TSMC remains cheap relative to its semi-peer group; the stock is even somewhat undervalued, considering its unique position with Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL). On a P/E basis, the stock is trading at 24.3x C2024, slightly higher than its ratio in late August at 23.1x but still comfortably lower than the peer group average of 31.9x. Solely considering real AI top-line growth exposure, it would actually make more sense for TSMC to be pricier than, for example, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which hasn't achieved the same AI-related sales but trades at a P/E ratio of 45.8x. We think TSMC will continue to provide a favorable risk-reward profile in FY25.

Wall Street recognizes TSMC's special position. Of the 17 analysts covering the stock, 16 are buy-rated, and the remaining one is hold-rated, which is similar to the sentiment we had when we wrote about the stock in late August. The stock is currently priced at $187 per share, with a median price target of $210 and a mean price target of $207 for a potential 10-12% upside. It's our belief that TSMC is positioned to outpace current median and mean sell-side price targets on its industry-go-to status and heightened demand for advanced nodes; the company has grown at a CAGR of 14% since 1998, reflecting just how much leverage TSMC has in the semi supply chain. The following charts outline TSMC's sell-side ratings and price targets.

What to do With the Stock?

Management is guiding Q4 sales between $26.1B and $26.9B, representing a 13% Q/Q and 35% Y/Y growth at the midpoint, and we expect TSMC to report within the upper range of that guidance. While the stock is up over 12% in Thursday trading so far, we see more upside in 4Q24 and FY25. The back end of the year tends to be more of a "peak season" for TSMC, as hyper scalers and other tech companies' stock up ahead of the end-of-year holidays. AI penetration remains low at mid-single-digit percentage, which most investors don't understand yet. So, as the AI penetration rate expands, we expect AI tailwinds for TSMC to expand alongside it.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Consumers To The Rescue Again
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify.

The consumer keeps spending, giving the Fed more leeway on the pace of cuts. (0:16) Taiwan Semi restores faith in AI chip demand. (1:58) Meta lays down the law on meal vouchers. (3:41)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

Our top story so far. A trifecta of macro reports that were soft-landing friendly—headlined by retail sales—are pushing Treasury yields higher.

September retail sales rose 0.4% from August, topping the +0.3% consensus and accelerating from +0.1% prior. On a Y/Y basis, retail sales climbed 1.7%. Core retail sales, which exclude motor vehicles and parts, increased 0.5% M/M, topping the +0.1% consensus.

The control group, which excluded autos and gas and feeds into GDP, jumped 0.7% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus. That’s the biggest rise in three months and the third-largest monthly gain of 2024.

Control group sales, which tends to line up with personal consumption spending in the GDP report, also came in much stronger than expected with a 0.7% gain. That is the biggest gain in three months and the third largest monthly gain of 2024.

Wells Fargo economists say that "even though retail sales have been choppy this year, there is an underlying theme that shows the composition of spending tending to favor those categories that feed into the Commerce Department's GDP calculations."

"The upshot is that despite hand-wringing over the state of the labor market, U.S. consumers just keep . spending."

Speaking of the labor market, weekly initial jobless claims fell as expected to 241,000, although claims are still seeing some impact from Hurricane Helene and the Boeing strike.

And the Philly Fed manufacturing index for October rose more than anticipated to 10.3.

Overall, the data reinforced the belief that the Fed doesn’t have to cut too rapidly. While two quarter-point rate cuts are still priced in, the odds of a December cut fell below 80%, and the odds of a cut in March moved to a toss-up.

Also on the central bank front, the ECB cut rates by another 25 basis points to 3.25% as inflation dipped below 2%.

Among active stocks, Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) jumped after the global foundry reported third-quarter results and guidance that topped estimates amid surging demand for AI chips. That took some of the sting out of ASML’s (ASML) disappointing bookings earlier this week.

CEO C.C. Wei says AI “demand is real, and I believe it's just the beginning,” adding that overall semiconductor demand has stabilized and has started to improve.

Wei also said Taiwan Semiconductor has no interest in buying Intel's (INTC) foundry business.

Looking ahead, revenue contribution from server AI processors is expected to more than triple this year and account for a mid-teens percentage of total revenue in 2024. Full-year revenue is forecast to rise 30% from the year ago.

Blackstone (BX) turned in better-than-expected Q3 earnings as investment activity picked up. Fund appreciation was its strongest in three years. Among its strategies, corporate private equity and infrastructure realized the best performances.

Total assets under management were $1.11 trillion, topping the Visible Alpha consensus of $1.09 trillion. Fee-earning assets under management rose to $820.5 billion from $808.7 billion at the end of Q2.

And Red Robin Gourmet Burgers (RRGB) is higher as JCP Investment Partnership increased its active stake to 11.3% from 8.7% through a series of transactions across several related buying parties. The firm sees the restaurant stock as undervalued and representing an attractive investment opportunity.

Shares of Red Robin have perked up over the last week, gaining more than 13%. Short interest on RRGB stands at 14.2% of the total float, which could add some volatility to the mix.

In other news of note, Meta Platforms (META) has initiated layoffs across various divisions, specifically targeting teams within WhatsApp, Instagram, and Reality Labs. That’s according to The Verge.

Although the exact number of job cuts has not been disclosed, reports indicate that they are relatively small in scale.

These layoffs follow a broader trend of workforce reductions at Meta, which has seen about 21,000 jobs cut since late 2022. That’s part of broader cost-cutting initiatives, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg labeling 2023 as the "year of efficiency." (It was also the year of the Rabbit.)

Also, the FT says Meta fired about two dozen staffers in its Los Angeles office for using their $25 meal credits to buy household items. Some employees used their $25 meal credit for items including acne pads, wine glasses, and laundry detergent.

Those who violated the company rules only on occasion were reprimanded but not terminated, but those who were fired were apparently abusing the food credit system over a long period of time, with some pooling their money together.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BMO strategist Brian Belski says the valuation backdrop for small-caps and mid-caps continues to look compelling.

Small caps are still trading at a discount relative to their 20-year average, while mid-caps trade only at a slight premium. The S&P 500 (SP500) trades at a significant premium relative to their 20-year average.

And the S&P 600 (SP600) and S&P 400 (SP400) have averaged double-digit gains in the one year following initial rate cuts by the Fed since 1995, at 12.8% and 10.7%, respectively.

“We believe peak pessimism is in place, making a rebound even more likely and recent trends have begun to show some improvement,” Belski said, adding “it is only a matter of time before the fortunes of this group take a turn for the better.”

Among the Outperform-rated stocks that fit a highly selected buying approach are Constellation Energy (CEG), Digital Realty Trust (DLR), Ross Stores (ROST), Snap (SNAP) and Take-Two Interactive (TTWO).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
